steps.title: Locate the word in context
steps.content: Clara says her plan will be "the best plan." The narrator says it was "an excellent plan, no doubt, and very neatly and simply arranged;" "Simply" modifies "arranged" and pairs with "neatly," so it describes the quality of the arrangement — something tidy and uncomplicated.

steps.title: Test the meaning against the contrast that follows
steps.content: The narrator immediately notes that "the only difficulty was, that Clara had not the smallest idea how to set about it." The plan looked uncomplicated on paper even though carrying it out was hard. "Simply" therefore means "in an easy, uncomplicated way."

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — Faintly: would mean barely or weakly arranged, which makes no sense for a plan praised as excellent. Misreads the tone of "neatly and simply." ✗
  • Choice B — Hastily: suggests rushed planning, but the passage emphasizes neatness, not speed. Overreach beyond what the text supports. ✗
  • Choice C — Easily: matches "in a straightforward, uncomplicated manner," consistent with "neatly" and with the contrast that only the execution — not the plan itself — was hard. ✓
  • Choice D — Foolishly: contradicts the positive presentation of the plan as "the best plan" and "an excellent plan"; the narrator never calls the arrangement foolish. ✗
That matches Choice C — Easily.
